data.title: EWS: A Narrow Developed Market ETF, With A Strong Cyclical Tilt
data.description:
Introduction to the iShares MSCI Singapore ETF

The iShares MSCI Singapore ETF (EWS), which has been in existence since March 1996, currently covers 17 stocks from the equity markets of Singapore. EWS, which was created by iShares, the ETF arm of Blackrock, Inc.. It has collected around $850M of AUM since it got listed, and is priced at an expense ratio of 0.5%. This ETF pays dividends twice a year, with its distributions annualizing to a figure of 3.67% at the current market price.

What Does EWS Do?

EWS’s primary goal is to attempt to mirror the performance of an index called the MSCI Singapore 25/50 Index (MS25I). Note that MS25I covers the same 17 stocks as the more famous MSCI Singapore Index [MSI] which represents the top 85% of Singapore’s market-cap. However, where MS25I differs is that it imposes certain capping restrictions on its holdings to prevent concentration of group holdings.

So, the stake of any group entity is capped at 25%, and all group entities with weights of over 5% are jointly permitted to only account for 50% of the index. For instance, DBS Group Holdings Limited (OTCPK:DBSDY) which is the top stock for both indexes, enjoys a weight of over 25% in the MSI, but in MS25I it currently accounts for less than 21%.

What Are The Main Features of EWS’s Portfolio?

One of the most standout characteristics of EWS is that it is very narrow in scope, covering just 17 stocks in total, making this a highly concentrated bet by general ETF standards (most ETFs usually cover around 50-200 stocks on average).

Then, as noted in the previous section, EWS’s tracking index focuses on the top 85% of Singapore’s market-cap, which means you won’t get access to small-cap stocks. Also since this index is market-cap weighted (with some caps in place to limit group holdings concentration), giant and large-caps in aggregate account for over 80% of the portfolio.

From a sectoral basis, EWS doesn’t offer coverage to a plethora of sectors (naturally, with only 17 stocks there is only so much you can offer), and one sector in particular stands out. The financial sector accounts for almost 40% of the portfolio.

What Are The Risks Associated With EWS?

EWS may focus on SGD-denominated (Singaporean Dollar) stocks, but since it is a USD-based product, investors could end up taking a hit if the SGD depreciates against the USD, even if these stocks go up in local currency terms.

Then, we noted previously how EWS seeks to mirror the MS25I; well, you would think this wouldn’t be a great challenge given that EWS only needs to replicate 17 stocks and their respective weightings, but the tracking errors associated with this product, be it over the past 1, 3 or 5 years, suggests that it doesn’t do a particularly great job. Across all these durations, EWS’s tracking errors are wider than what most ETFs see (and most of these ETFs need to replicate a far larger pool of stocks amounting to hundreds and thousands of stocks, and not just 17 stocks). Thus, for a narrowly-focused passively managed ETF, we don’t feel EWS does a good job.

The other major risk is the heightened degree of top-heaviness; just the top two stocks of EWS- DBSDY, and SEA Limited (SE) account for over one-third of this portfolio, so it would be rather challenging for EWS to flourish if these two stocks are staring at weak prospects.

Also note that the top four sectors (which account for 87% of the portfolio), are all cyclical sectors, which makes EWS quite vulnerable to amplified swings during a down cycle in the overall economy. The challenge with orienting yourself around cyclical sectors is that one also has to be very well-versed with the health of various economic indicators and be very good at timing and calling a bottom or a top. Besides Singapore’s stature as a global financial hub and its strong inter-connectedness with the global economy also makes it sensitive to weak economic hues beyond just its geographic terrain.

Investors may also want to consider that EWS currently doesn’t offer any exposure to the technology sector, which in recent years, has proven to be a magnet for attracting investment flows. If you’re big on the prospects of AI penetration across the globe, pursuing something like an EWS won’t provide the necessary uplift.

In addition to that, it is also concerning to note that EWS doesn’t offer sufficient defensive power which could come in handy during periods of risk aversion. Note that its exposure to defensive sectors such as consumer staples and utilities is very minimal at less than 8% in aggregate, whereas it doesn’t dabble with another defensive sector at all (healthcare).

Who Is EWS ETF For?

Singapore has developed a reputation around the world for its business-friendly policies, particularly for fostering an ideal ecosystem for financial businesses to flourish. Thus, it helps that EWS is over indexed to this sector alone which accounts for almost 40% of the portfolio. A portfolio tilted to financial stocks also means that it is likely to score quite strongly on the income front, and we can see with that with EWS’s overall income profile.

EWS’s yield tends to come in at around the 4% levels, and the current yield of 3.69% is a good 100bps more than what the median ETF offers. Also consider that EWS’s most recent semi-annual distribution was up by an impressive 31% YoY. It helps that EWS’s top holding- DBSDY (which alone has a 21% weight) has become increasingly generous with its dividend payouts. Going forward through FY27 DBSDY plans to share around $5B with its shareholders in the form of capital return dividends (15 cents per quarter) as well as ordinary dividends (66 cents per quarter),

Investors who appreciate GARP-style stocks (Growth at a reasonable price) or stocks that are available at reasonable valuations yet offer decent enough growth prospects, will likely take a fancy to EWS, as around two-thirds of its portfolio consists of blended-type stocks.

We would also view EWS as an ideal developed market diversifier for those looking to reduce exposure to the tech-heavy pricey US markets, which many would argue looks over-heated at this juncture. We say this because EWS’s beta is relatively low (compared to other major developed markets besides the UK) at 0.59x (what this means is that for every $1 movement in the S&P 500, EWS should move far less, by an estimated $0.59).

EWS is also cut off from the tech sector, so in case you’re worried about the AI bubble bursting, EWS may be better prepared to take the hits. Also it is priced at a P/E (price-to-earnings) multiple of only 15x, and a P/CF (price-to-cash flow) multiple of only 13x, which should position it favorably to value-investors who want to move out of US stocks (the S&P 500 is priced at a P/E premium of 53% and a P/CF premium of 24%).

What Alternatives Are There Besides EWS?

Well, unfortunately, EWS is the only US-listed ETF vehicle that provides pure-play exposure to the Singaporean equity universe. However, if you’re open to looking at another quasi-alternative that offers meaningful enough exposure to Singaporean stocks, but isn’t a pure-play product, one could also consider the Global X FTSE Southeast Asia ETF (ASEA).

As the name suggests ASEA, covers a diversified portfolio of Southeast Asian stocks, of which Singaporean equities account for the bulk (46% in total, which is 2.4x the next largest regional exposure- Thai equities). Like EWS, ASEA too is passively managed and follows an index called the FTSE/ASEAN 40 Index [F4I] which focuses on the 40 largest stocks from five different Southeast Asian markets including Singapore.

What are the pluses and minuses of choosing ASEA over EWS?

Well, let’s start with the pluses. Firstly, you get wider reach and more diversification to higher-growth terrains. With EWS, you’re looking at a paltry total of just 17 stocks, whereas ASEA covers over 40 stocks. With the exception of Thailand, all these other developing economies from South East Asia, also have a much superior growth profile than Singapore.

Despite covering more higher-growth regions ASEA’s holdings are also priced at a lower price-to-earnings multiple of 13x, which represents a double-digit discount over EWS’s corresponding multiple of nearly 15x.

Then, ASEA’s greater diversification effects and wider reach also plays a part in ASEA’s average monthly returns seeing a lesser degree of variation than EWS.

ASEA is also a bit more stable, churning only 11% of its holdings every year; that is roughly only half as much as the annual churn that EWS witnesses (higher churn means higher transaction costs)

Then we previously highlighted EWS’s troubling price performance since it got listed three decades ago. Well do note that even since ASEA got listed in February 2011, our product in focus has consistently underperformed its younger peer, with price appreciation that has only been one-third of what ASEA has seen (admittedly even ASEA has hardly set the world alight with a price appreciation of only 18% in 14 years)!

Now coming to the drawbacks of choosing ASEA over EWS, we first have to flag the very concerning levels of liquidity. ASEA only sees around $350K of dollar volumes per day, which is not even 2% of the volumes that EWS sees every day. Such low daily volumes also means that the bid and the ask associated with ASEA tends to be very widely spread out (in percentage terms it amounts to a whopping 0.68%), and that is not the case with EWS, whose bid-ask spread is very respectable for a foreign geography ETF at 0.03%. Those wide spreads associated with ASEA makes it vulnerable to high risk of slippage, or getting prices that are highly divergent from what was initially budgeted before taking a position. Alternatively, if you’re prepared to be patient, you may have to wait a long time before you get a good fill on your trade.

EWS is also a more cost-efficient product with an expense ratio that is 15bps lower than ASEA. While both these products distribute dividends twice a year, EWS also appears to offer a higher figure than ASEA at current prices. It’s also fair to say that while ASEA may offer good geographical diversification, from a sectoral perspective, it is over indexed to the financial sector, which alone accounts for almost 60% of the portfolio. This makes it very vulnerable to interest rate and cyclical shifts.

Summary

EWS the only US-listed ETF to offer pure-play exposure to Singaporean stocks. It is a very narrow, income-oriented ETF with a very strong tilt towards cyclical stocks and the financial sector in particular. EWS’s long-term price performance is a source of concern, and it also offers limited defensive sector exposure, but alternatively it could also work as a useful diversifier for developed market investors looking to move away from the tech-heavy and pricey US markets.

This article answers these three main questions about EWS:

  • What are the main features of the EWS portfolio?
  • What type of investor will appreciate EWS, and what are the risks they could face?
  • How does EWS stack up against a more diversified ETF basket of Southeast Asian equities?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Jack In The Box: Everything Has Been Priced In, But No Drivers (Downgrade)
data.description:
Ever since I began trading in the U.S. market, Jack in the Box Inc. (JACK) has been my most unsuccessful attempt. From my previous coverage last March, the stock has already plunged by over 40%. And that was understandable considering inflationary headwinds, fierce restaurant competition, and futile attempts to rebound or at least improve margins. The result? Its disposal of Del Taco. To be honest, I’ve already seen the red flags but thought they were part of the cyclical market. But as market aspects became more challenging, I’ve come to realize that JACK could not insulate itself as much as its peers I’ve also covered did.

At this point, valuation is already very cheap, especially if we account for the impact of the Del Taco sales. However, it may still be challenging to justify upside. Technicals still adhere to this as bearish signals remain visible.

Q4 2025: Resilience Remained, But Market Dynamics Hammered It

Even now, inflation has not presented any indication of mellowing down. Although it has improved from its skyrocketing trend in 2022 and 2023, prices are still generally increasing. This continues to affect purchasing power and pricing flexibility. Jack in the Box, Inc. was no exception despite its established brand recognition and domestic market presence.

During Q4 2025, its operating revenue amounted to $326.2M, down by 6.6% YoY from $349.3M. All its revenue components decreased YoY, showing that both company-operated and franchised restaurants weakened. There were also store closures amid weakening demand. If we concentrate on its company-operated restaurants, the average sales per restaurant were $505,373 ($142.5M/282 restaurants). This was still lower than in the previous year with $535,042 ($151.4/283 restaurants).

In addition, it still had a hard time managing cost pressures despite reducing its total restaurant count (company-operated and franchisees). To be fair, the YoY difference came from the impact of the disposal of company-operated restaurants. If we exclude it, the total operating costs and expenses will be lower at $302.8M versus $302.83M YoY. As you can see, most components decreased YoY. The increase mainly came from SG&A expenses. But overall, this was still not enticing since the total number of restaurants has already decreased by 73 units. As a result, its operating margin dropped to 7.3%, half of that in Q4 2024 at 14.6%. The consolation was that JACK remained profitable and that its decision to reduce its restaurant count helped it cope with the intensifying cost pressures.

Challenges

JACK remains highly sensitive to inflation since it had to trim down its restaurants to cope with the increasing cost pressures. It also has to deal with lower purchasing power and softer demand, which continue to affect its pricing strategies and margins. Now, it’s back to its 14-month high of 3.0%. This means that prices are still increasing. This may be aggravated by new tariffs due to their inflationary nature. The price of its ingredients and raw materials may also rise, which can further squeeze its profits. This will also be more challenging for low and lower-middle-income households to maintain their discretionary spending. In fact, they can hardly cope with the rising fast-food prices, so fast-food traffic from low-income households has already dropped by double-digits. Even a larger player like McDonald’s (MCD) is losing its low-income customers.

Another challenge is the stiff competition. MCD declared another war after cutting the prices of its combo meals. U.S. company-operated restaurants and franchisees agreed to reduce prices by 15%. It also expanded its combo offering to reintroduce its Extra Value Meals branding with a $5 breakfast deal and an $8 Big Mac and Nugget special. I find this move bold and risky for MCD. Yet, it can be predatory to its competitors. Amid the current market dynamics, it seems that competition has become survival of the fittest. And whoever survives may capture the demand of the losing contender. This is another threat that JACK may face.

Moreover, fast-food restaurants may face indirect competition with fast-casual diners. As fast-food brands try to cope with inflationary headwinds, they may have to raise prices. And fast-casual diners are known to capitalize on value propositions like premium ingredients rather than cheaper pricing. Typically, their menu items are slightly set at premium prices. Because fast-food prices increase, their gap decreases. So, customers may face a choice between fast-food and fast-casual restaurants.

Del Taco Sale: Some Opportunities

JACK entered into a definitive agreement with Yadav to sell Del Taco for $115M in cash. And I think this is a sound move considering its continued weakness. During Q4 2025, its average systemwide sales per restaurant were only $361,270 ($208.14M/576 restaurants) versus $370,272M. This means that actual demand and pricing remained weak regardless of the restaurant count. For the whole year, the average sales per restaurant dropped to $1.59B versus $1.61B YoY.

Selling Del Taco means JACK will lose a huge portion of its sales. But it also means it can manage costs and expenses better. It can also concentrate better on its main brand, which can help it improve efficiency and pricing/marketing strategies. It won’t have to compete with much larger competitors like Taco Bell (QSR) and Chipotle (CMG).

Lastly, selling it will add liquidity to JACK. I understand that JACK maintains decent liquidity with its robust cash flow. For the whole year, its operating cash flow amounted to $162.4M, which was enough to cover capex, debt repayments, share repurchases, and dividends. So, its cash level more than doubled in just a year.

However, its borrowings remain high. Although a huge portion has a maturity of more than one year, its Net Debt/EBITDA remains high at 7.4x. This means that JACK needs seven years to repay the whole amount at once even if it doesn’t have to. Over $1B will mature within five years, which is lower than the ratio. This means that JACK has a limited capital buffer to sustain its current capacity and dividends. Hence, disposing of Del Taco may be a wise move.

Valuation

I believe that its current valuation has already priced in the sale of Del Taco. I will explain the technicals more later.

As I looked into its price ratios, I noticed that the stock price has been more consistent with the trend of cash and free cash flow. So, I used these financial metrics to assess the stock price. The price has clearly decreased along with cash per share and FCF per share, so the continued downtrend was justifiable. Yet, it appears that the actual price decrease appears a bit too stretched. Using P/FCF, the JACK trades at only 4.92x its free cash flow versus the five-year average of 13.52x. If we use this to get the fair value, the TP or target price will be $39.48. The same applies to P/CPS, which suggests a TP of $41.87. These ratios imply that the price decreased more than necessary relative to previous years.

However, we should note the potential impact of Del Taco, which can reduce its FCF and other important financial metrics like sales. Also, there are still no growth catalysts to support its growth or rebound. To have a more forward-looking approach, I used the Dividend Discount Model. Note that its annualized dividend of $0.88 is based on its payouts in the most recent quarters. Because it has already cut dividends, I set its dividend growth rate at 0.00%. Meanwhile, I set its risk premium at 5.0%. Lastly, I set its Beta at 1.00 to be consistent with the direction of the S&P 500. Using all these numbers, I derived a TP of $9.66.

Considering the disagreement between the two methods, I advise investors to be cautious and stay on the sidelines. But if we decide to trust price ratios more than the Dividend Discount Model, I believe that it will have a very hard time getting to that level or at least to where it was when I covered it. But I think a buyout is the best scenario for valuation to increase at this point.

Technical Analysis

JACK’s technicals remain bearish as the price moves with lower highs and lows, which suggests a downtrend continuation. The price also moves below the 50-day and 200-day SMA, which shows that recent price momentum has still weakened relative to the average trend in the past 50 and 200 days. It is also worth noting that the price decreased again after consolidating for a few months. This means that more sellers opted to go out, which could have been fueled by the decision to sell Del Taco. In addition, the 50-day SMA is still trending downward, which shows that market sentiments are still pessimistic.

Its MACD and RSI consistently move with the price, so we cannot detect any bullish divergence. The MACD line is still decreasing below the signal line, which means that selling volume is still increasing. This is also within negative territory along with the histogram, which implies that selling volume still outpaces buying volume. Meanwhile, RSI shows potential overselling. Despite this, it doesn’t assure investors of a rebound as the price trend remains weak.

Conclusion

Jack in the Box, Inc. remains hammered and does not indicate recovery amid stubborn inflation and predatory competition. The sale of Del Taco may open opportunities to improve its margins and liquidity, but it will be hard for it to regain its previous position. Today, valuation makes sense and has already priced in risks and disposal opportunities but remains almost fully valued. Technicals are also bearish amid a weak outlook despite the potential overselling. I’m downgrading my rating on this stock from Buy to Hold.

data.title: REITs Are Boring And Boring Is Good
data.description:
Listen here or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Hoya Capital's David Auerbach on past REIT underperformance evolving into solid fundamentals across the board (1:00). Interest rates and REITs (5:20). Contextualizing dividends (10:20). Alexandria and other earnings season takeaways (15:40). Retail REITs and the US economy (20:55). Office REITs (27:40). Hoya Capital ETFs HOMZ and RIET (30:30). The most misunderstood thing about REITs (38:05).

Transcript

Rena Sherbill: Welcome back, David Auerbach, Chief Investment Officer at Hoya Capital. Great to have you back on Investing Experts talking REITs. Welcome back to the show, David.

David Auerbach: Great to see you. A lot sure changed since we last visited at the Hilton Midtown not that long ago, it seems. And what a crazy environment that we're operating in these days.

Rena Sherbill: What a crazy environment. You're referring of course to our Investing Summit, which was a fantastically informative time back in June of last year.

So bring us up to date. We've seen a lot of underperformance in the previous years from the REIT space. There's some promise of some higher performing stocks when we look at that space. What are you thinking about? What are you looking at? And maybe bring us up to date about how investors should be thinking contextually about the REIT space.

David Auerbach: A lot to spend right there in that one question we could probably spend an hour just on that alone. Let's just take a step back here.

If you look back to when the Fed started hiking interest rates back in March of 2022, the REITs have underperformed the S&P (SP500) by about 70 percent. So to say that that's a massive sell off is an understatement. If you go back to the global financial crisis under performance, that was around 20 percent.

So as a result, REITs are no doubt very cheap, but it does constrain them because it means that there could be high costs of capital. External growth is somewhat difficult to come by. However, if you peel back the layers of the onion, what you'll see is that by and large, fundamentals are very solid across the board.

If you take the whole REIT asset classes, I like to say in conversations, the REIT sector is like your graduating class of high school. You have the top 10%, the REITs that are in the S&P 500, the valedictorian, the salutatorian of your class. Then you have the other side of the curve, the bottom 10%, the bullies, the troublemakers, the REITs that cut their dividends or the REITs that are over levered.

But what we like to do is focus on that middle part of the curve, the 150 students that go under the radar that don't draw attention, cause conflicts or anything. And that's really in the small and mid cap REITs.

That's where you're going to see some of these opportunities, especially when you look towards more yielding income oriented investments, where we do see those opportunities.

One additional point to note here is that this volatility provides opportunity. And if you take a step back looking outside of REITs and figure out, pick your favorite stock, we could use buzzworthy stocks like Nvidia (NVDA), or Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT), whatever stocks that you're looking at.

The end investor wants a company to do four things at the end of the day, really four things. Grow revenues, grow profits, grow dividends, and grow annual guidance. If a company is able to do those four things, regardless of what macroeconomic headlines are being thrown their way, you would assume that that company is on solid footing.

Now, like every other sector that's out there sure there's a couple of bad apples as part of this entire bunch but the broad entire scope of the universe most of those four things are happening on a quarter after quarter basis here's how to look at this we just wrapped up third quarter earnings and i know we're going to get into it but if the companies that provide 2025 earnings guidance, more than two thirds have raised their guidance.

More than a third of these REITs have raised their dividends this year. And more than 60 % of the companies raised their net operating income guidance for this year. And this is in the wake of tariffs rising at our high interest rates, a 10-year treasury that continues to remain elevated, treasury rates remains elevated. All of these macro factors are being thrown at the company.

So at the end of the day, an investor wants a management team, whether it's Jensen Huang at Nvidia, Tim Cook at Apple, or David Simon at Simon Property Group. They want that management team to put the blinders on and focus on ways on how they can grow revenues while cutting expenses in the hopes of maximizing profit or net income that is passed through to shareholders in the form of dividends.

Rena Sherbill: I wanna get to the dividends in a second. I'm curious though, because of this rate environment and this questioning, by the way, for a deeper dive into the question of how the rate environment is affecting REITs, on December 9th, you are going to be part of a webinar with Daniel Snyder. So look for that, that's at 2 p.m. Eastern on December 9th.

But in this rate interest conversation where there's the promise of lowered interest rates after a couple of years of higher interest rates, how does that figure in and how does that play into how you strategize looking at REITs in general? And then in terms of the dividends, how does that affect things?

David Auerbach: A great question and you know from a very high level I want to start out by saying there's this misconception that I like to say that REITs are interest rate sensitive.

Going back to what you learned in your finance class we were always taught that REITs are kind of like that contra beta to the market as in when the market is up REITs are down when the market is down REITs are up and now coming out of COVID for the past few years it seems like everything trades a lockstep with each other.

Number two, when they say that REITs are interest rate sensitive, I call BS on that because really when I think about REITs, I think about the end consumers that use these REIT-owned properties every single day.

Case in point, we're recording this session right now that's going to be posted to the cloud or viewed on YouTube or various different sites. For the end user to watch that, they're going to be utilizing a data center REIT to get that content is that user thinking about where the 10 year treasury rate is what the fed is doing with interest rates today as they're watching this interview down the road and the answer is of course not if a read is out there actively developing a property they're in the market for a construction loan they're in the market to take down debt from Goldman Sachs or JP Morgan then yes, obviously the current market impacts the interest rate on the note that they're taking down.

But I think one other key piece that is kind of getting overlooked is that many of these REITs have, you know, investment grade ratings from a lot of the ratings agencies, Moody's, S&P, Fitch. So they know what the borrowing environment looks like.

In a given day, the CFO knows what the lending environment looks like. And can I borrow your paper today at five, 5.05, 5.1. It's not like that that window moves very much on a day to day basis. Again, it's in those times of volatility where you see those big jumps. But let's get back to your question.

We know that there is going to be a more, we'll say dovish incoming Fed chairperson next year. We don't know who yet, but we know that the goal is to bring in somebody that's going to be much more favorable and let's say aggressive in cutting interest rates. Therefore, those tailwinds should benefit the REITs sector as that happens. But it's more than that. REITs are compared to fixed income.

Thus why REIT yields are always closely scrutinized versus the 10-year treasury because they kind of trade in lockstep with each other. So if the average REIT dividend yield is around 4 % right now, while the 10-year treasury trades north of 4%, that would technically make fixed income a more attractive investment vehicle. But if you, there's a couple different ways to look at this.

That 4% number that I'm giving you is basically a market cap weighted number. So the biggest of the biggest reads, the American Towers (AMT) of the world, the Prologises (PLD) of the world, frankly, aren't yielding that much, which is why here at Hoya, again, we find the value in the small and mid cap reads moving down that ladder.

So if you equal cap weight all of those reads, that yield goes towards around 6% or so. And thus, again, on an equal basis, REITs would be more attractive than fixed income. And as Fed cuts rates, one would hope the 10-year Treasury number goes lower as well. And thus, that spread between REIT dividends and fixed income would continue to widen.

Rena Sherbill: Speaking of dividends, we saw a bunch of dividend hikes in October that we just got that report out recently. What would you say to your point about dividends and that there should be improved reasons to be bullish on REITs? What would you say contextually about the dividend hikes that we've seen?

David Auerbach: I want to bring in dividends with other things that a lot of the REITs have been doing to unlock value. Again, if we go back to the start of 2022, when the Fed started hiking interest rates, we've seen 36 different companies that have basically gone away, merged, liquidated, explored options, trying to unlock value.

In the past quarter alone, we've seen 10 different REITs go through that same process. While going back to 2022, we've only seen 10 new REITs come to market.

So at this point, management teams are trying to figure out ways to unlock value for shareholders since most of these REITs are trading at discounts to net asset value. But to answer your question about dividends, that's why this kind of plays hand in hand with what I was just explaining. Because if you go back to pre-COVID, right before COVID started, again, and I know we're talking about something from five years ago, but a lot of these sectors are still compared about how are they performing versus pre-COVID.

And the way to say about the REITs was, before COVID, the REITs were basically at the top of the market. Leasing wasn't an all-time high, rental rates were an all-time high, dividends were at an all-time high. The REIT sector was just firing on all cylinders. COVID hits, REIT sector goes in the tank for a variety of reasons. Fed starts hiking interest rates. REITs are out of favor.

But let's go to the fundamental side of the equation. Here we are five years later. The REITs are putting up fundamentals that surpass what they were doing at their COVID peak, their earnings, their growth, but their dividends have not caught up to where they were pre-COVID for a variety of reasons. But I would say,

One of the specific reasons as to why a lot of the REITs have not gotten so aggressive on hiking is because I feel like some of them are retaining cash for a rainy day. Is it better to raise my dividend by a penny or two pennies across, again, millions of shares of stock, or is it better to retain that capital to find the right opportunity that comes along?

Most, if not almost every single one of these companies, their earnings that they're making are dramatically covering their dividend payment versus a handful of options where, excuse me, a handful of companies where the dividend is not being covered by earnings.

So the takeaway here is that the REITs that continue to grow still have ample capacity to continue to raise their dividends.

Rena Sherbill: And what names would you put to that? What names would you put in that column and what names would you put in the other column?

David Auerbach: It's kind of hard to forecast who will continue and all that type of stuff. It's very hard to kind of gauge. But a couple of that would stand out.

A good example would be like Simon Property Group (SPG). Simon just raised their dividend. I believe it's for the 11th time coming out of COVID. That's one example.

You have companies like Welltower (WELL) that have grown their market cap by over 5x in the past half decade and they're one of the very, very few REIT stocks that's actually trading at a premium right now.

I think it's really on a case by case, sector by sector basis. Another company that stands out as far as dividend growth is a small cap health care REIT called Strawberry Fields (STRW).

Strawberry Fields is a company that's more than doubled the size of their portfolio. And frankly, their dividend has doubled in size as well. I think a good way to look at dividend growth is looking towards the companies and the fundamentals and seeing how they are growing.

Meaning, if we have gone out and acquired, I'm just throwing random numbers out there. If we've increased our portfolio revenue by 25, 50%, dot, dot, dot, one would assume that the dividend is going to go up by a corresponding amount as well. So really look towards the internal and external growth of these companies and their earnings to kind of get a gauge on what's being pushed through.

Then there's other companies that pay consistent monthly dividends, Realty Income (O), Agree Realty (ADC). The list of the companies are out there, but it's slow and steady, tried and true.

I interviewed the CEO of AGNC Mortgage (AGNC), a mortgage REIT just a couple of months ago, and he was very transparent saying, look, David, we're a mortgage REIT. Mortgage REITs go under the radar. They're always kind of under the gun.

And guess what? We've never cut our dividend. We've maintained our monthly dividend through COVID and beyond. We're plotting along because our customers are Fannie and Freddie and government-backed securities. And so we're able to continue to do business as usual.

So I think we could play this on a case-by-case, company-by-company level. I will say, again, to be fair and balanced, one company to keep an eye on that might cut their dividend, and they're kind of implying as much, would be Alexandria (ARE).

They had a very disappointing third quarter earnings report. And they are kind of guiding towards a 2026 dividend cut. If you read through some of the materials they've published here in the past few weeks.

Rena Sherbill: What would you say from earnings season? What left you surprised? What were you expecting? And anything else that you feel like would be valuable takeaways from earnings season for investors to hear.

David Auerbach: Sure, I would say obviously that Alexandria report was probably the biggest surprise of all of the REITs that reported during earning season. Period, we could put a bow on that.

As far as sectors, looking sectors on a case by case basis, one sector that continues to jump out is senior housing. Senior housing is a sector that is gonna continue to do very, very well. It's obviously still coming out of the COVID cycle when senior living was very much under appreciated because of what was going on.

But the we're seeing very limited new supply with a sector that's seeing robust rent growth. So that's one sector that jumps out. Obviously, there's this two letter phrase that's kicked around a billion times a day called AI. And so AI beneficiaries will clearly be data centers.

That's a sector that's continued to hum along. In fact, we just saw this recent IPO of Fermi (FRMI) and it'll be a great walking case study to see how much does the data center reed buzz continue to burn into '26.

Retail, another good sector that's been standing out. I point to guys like Tanger (SKT). I also mentioned Simon earlier.

Some of these players are seeing double digit rent spreads, double digit rent spreads, frankly, because we haven't seen much new development in a while.

Another name that came out during the earnings cycle came from a non-REIT. It was Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) (BRK.B). As we know, Warren Buffett is leaving, retiring, and in one of their last quarterly earnings reports, they mentioned taking a stake in Lamar advertising company (LAMR), a billboard REIT.

What does Buffett know about billboards? And, that's again, a new type of industry where you don't see a lot of new supply. You don't see really many new billboards. Again, huge freestanding billboards being placed on the ground.

And then again, one other sector we thought that was a pretty good was mortgage REITs, as I mentioned, we're not really seeing much issue on the residential credit side. The commercial credit continues to remain status quo.

Any of those delinquency situations that might be happening, those are getting worked out. If you go to the other side of the coin, sectors that probably didn't perform as well as I mentioned, lab space, that would be the Alexandria story. Cold storage being impacted, obviously, by tariffs. There's a competition situation there.

We might be seeing a little bit some oversupply. So that's a sector that's going to continue to get a lot of focus. You know, we're seeing on the cell tower sites, some consolidation with Dish and EchoStar. Plus there's this thing called Starlink that's out there, obviously, which may impact some of the cell tower reads. Apartments have had some very mixed results. We're seeing three year flat apartment rent growth.

There are signs we might be seeing a lift into next year, but obviously still too soon to tell. And then, some of those that straddle the fence from good sides, some bad sides. Some of those sectors include office, industrial, hotels is a sector that's especially worth a conversation, gaming REITs, and then manufactured housing.

And I would say obviously, a lot of that earnings season volatility, especially stuff like hotels, gaming, and manufactured housing could be attributed to the travel slowdown, the government shutdown, Canada, lack of international tourism, things like that. So there's a lot again, earnings season really though, by and large was relatively mute.

As I mentioned, more than two thirds raise their annual guidance this year, a lot of them raise their net operating income guidance. And again, that's what you're kind of looking for. It's pretty much on par with one or two out of the ordinary results.

Again, Alexandria, one other REIT would be office properties, income trust, OPI, which basically is exploring bankruptcy.

Rena Sherbill: What would you say when it comes to the retail REITs, what would you say about the economy, how it's developing, how it's not developing, and how much that is putting, or how much that is having an effect on those REITs?

David Auerbach: That's a great question. And so there's a couple of different ways to look at this because when you talk about retail, there's frankly many different sectors of retail, right? You've got malls, you've got strip centers, you've also got call it some of those net lease assets that are retail focused. So you can kind of take it from across three different ways.

When it comes to the world of retail, when I think of grocery anchored shopping centers, they're going to command a premium because we're going to the grocery store every single week to buy groceries. Obviously, tariffs, inflation, that stuff plays into it as well, but we're still going to the grocery store to get down take down groceries on the let's say mall and retail component of it.

The high end is outweighing the low end. The luxury properties continue to do well. The best in class operators, the Simons of the world continue to hum along. They're seeing opportunities. I think there's interesting stories out there to follow when you look at like the resurgence of CBL Properties (CBL), a REIT that was in bankruptcy a couple of years ago that is coming out again on the other side.

Macerich (MAC) in the process of a turnaround situation in itself. But I think the key when you look at at least on the mall and retail side, then I turned to Tanger (SKT), Steven Yaloff, their CEO, just go into Google, type in Steven Yaloff, Tanger, and you will see, I'm not exaggerating, every single week he's either on Bloomberg, on CNBC, or on some news channel highlighting what's going on at their properties.

Where I'm going with this is that when you look towards a lot of these companies, they really are focusing on the experiential offering side of the equation. We don't want to get showroomed.

A good example here in Dallas at one of our most famous mall properties, Netflix (NFLX) is opening an entertainment concept here any day now. And so it's a way to drive foot traffic to the property, especially a family.

How do we bring the family to the mall for several hours and capture them, whether it's through a movie, through American Girl or Build-A-Bear or Netflix or Crayola or Meow Wolf or whatever it is. The goal is to try to find ways to draw families to the property, but more importantly, so that we don't really showroom that product.

Why do I need to go to JCPenney when I can go to JCPenney, look at the item of clothing, and then go buy it online at home or something like that. You look at this shift.

I was just talking about Warby Parker (WRBY) with somebody last week when I was in New York. And Warby Parker as an online retailer shifting to a bricks and mortar concept.

Look at Tesla (TSLA). Tesla with the bricks and mortar concept, though you still can pretty much, I think, only order the car online, though I don't own a Tesla. So I think it's this interesting hybrid mix of looking at tried and true retailers that resonate with families while offering experiential offerings that can't be beat.

Go look at a Tanger property and all the different concepts that are around in terms of food, beverage, retail, experience, rides, etc. So that that family is able to go hang out those properties. And then on the net lease side, you know, that's an interesting angle to look at, at least on the net lease retail side, because there's so many different moving parts with that look at all the retail players that make up that sector. Obviously, gas stations, convenience stores, Subway, Starbucks, the list goes on and on.

And I think you can kind of look towards some of those earnings reports from some of those various retailers to see the strength of really what's going on there. I think one thing that kind of gets lost in the shuffle, many investors look at day-to-day performance of REITs.

And I think that's a bad idea because with the exception of hotels, which is technically a one night lease, the minimum lease term in place is an apartment lease, which is one year. But an office lease, a retail lease, et cetera, is five years, 10 years or more, all the way up to go out and buy safe hold, which focuses on ground leases, and that could be a 99 year lease.

So when I talk to people about the REIT industry, I always say, ignore the stock price story of this equation and focus on again this income side of it this long-term lease that's in place with x company with a set rent bump in place every single year here's this year's rent next year the year after the year after the year after we revisit it here and renegotiate so because of that steady rent bump growth that's kind of trying to build in some of that growth of that dividend

Part two, talking about REITs, you focus on the dividend income machine, that compounding of dividends. Look at Realty Income (O). If you bought one share of realty income five, 10, 25 years ago, reinvesting your dividends that get paid every single month and what you would have today versus where you started out when you bought that one share a few years ago.

When I talked about this with investors, I always say, you have to look at this sector with 25 to 50 year glasses. How much income are you going to earn by owning Realty Income over the course of the next 25 years as they consistently continue to grow that dividend?

So ignore the day-to-day volatility of the market and focus on that dividend check that you're getting every month, quarter, year, et cetera, from these companies. And any upside that you get on stock price, that's basically the extra cherry on top of the sundae.

Rena Sherbill: I'm curious, you mentioned senior living as a theme to look at after COVID. What about the office space? Has that seen an uptick or an evolution since the end of the COVID time?

David Auerbach: Frankly, the answer is both. You are seeing an evolution. Return to office is here in some form, but work from home is also still here. New York City will never go back, as far as I know, to a five days a week in the office, unless you're in a couple of different sectors, let's say.

You can go out and buy the most famous office building in the world, the Empire State Building (ESRT), for $7 a share right now. And I don't know anything, but something tells me that if you add up all the assets that Empire State Realty Trust owns, my gut tells me it's worth more than $7 a share.

The best of the best assets that are out there, especially when you look at some of the really trophy-type names. The SL Greens (SLG) of the world, the (BXP)s, the Highwoods (HIW), the Cousins (CUZ), some of these, again, these operators that own the best assets at the best intersections, the best markets, they're going to weather the storm and they're gonna be A-OK.

I'm more concerned about the smaller cap, over levered REIT in an undesirable suburban office that's 35% occupied. What do you do? Or a 1950s era class C office in New York City with asbestos, et cetera, what does that operator do?

I really think it's on a case by case basis, but in some markets, some of these companies are weathering the storm, frankly, a lot better than others. And I think one key to look at would be also maybe looking at some of the other players out there like a Blackstone (BX).

Seeing what they're doing on the private side with some of their office investments that they own. Because again, that could be seeing some moving parts.

And we're obviously seeing some interest in that sector because you have two office streets that may be going away or in the process of liquidating, including Paramount (PGRE), and City Office (CIO). But then there's other stories that are out there. Brandywine (BDN), Piedmont (PDM). Hudson Pacific (HPP), Douglas Emmett (DEI). There are some really interesting stories to follow out there to kind of see how office is evolving.

Rena Sherbill: I mentioned at the beginning that your chief investment officer at Hoya Capital, you have the investing group, iREIT and Hoya Capital. You also have two ETFs, (HOMZ) and (RIET). Would you share with listeners about those ETFs, how you decide which names go in them and what you've taken away from the ETFs thus far?

David Auerbach: I can talk about some there's some I cannot because we're currently in a blackout period for our quarterly rebalance. So I do want to be careful with how I present some of this. First of all, our funds are rules driven. I like to say that we're actively passive though we only make updates to it twice a year on our rebounds.

We are writing research and covering these companies every single day of the week. So we know these companies and management teams and interacting with them all the time. So we're actively covering them. But when you look at HOMZ, we feel it's the only true holistic way to cover all things housing. HOMZ was brought to market because when we brought it in 2019, there wasn't a true housing ETF.

You had a homebuilders ETF, you had a residence storage ETF, but there wasn't one that was covering everything housing. When I say everything housing, yes, that also includes the apartment reads, the single family rental reads, but then it's also the homebuilders. It's the home goods and supplies and furnishings, all the retailers that make up the home industry.

And then all the service providers, like your Zillow and Coldwell Banker and Compass and brokers and stuff. So it's a great way to understand truly what is going on in the world of housing.

And for those that care, it's no surprise. Housing is basically upside down right now. And the key point is home prices are unaffordable to the average consumer. Interest rates remain elevated. We aren't seeing a real tap into the amount of demand that's out there.

And so as a result, it's really the rental players, the apartments, the single-family rental guys that benefit in that topsy turvy environment. And until your favorite home builder, D.R. Horton (DHR) -Lennar (LEN), pick your favorite guy, basically issues a product that costs a third to half of what is currently being offered.

It's going to be some time until we see those demand numbers start to drop. And again, we think it's a lot of levers that need to be pulled. A 50-year mortgage isn't the answer. A portable mortgage isn't the answer. A manufactured housing might be one way to eat into that, but I feel like there's a lot of things that need to line up for us to see improvement on that side. And it all starts again with interest rates and seeing those come down.

RIET is the other end of it. That's the income side. So as a result, we're looking at the REITs that pay the highest dividends with the lowest leverage ratios. We try to find the REITs that really, again, line up in this rules-driven process where we think that their dividends are going to continue to grow or maintain in this volatile environment.

We skew it though towards small and mid cap REITs again, because that's where those yieldier opportunities arise. And we have exposure to some of the other unique sectors like mortgage REITs. And I believe it's still currently the only real estate ETF on the market that combines both common and preferred stocks. So 10 % of RIET or one third of the names of that hundred are in liquid.

REIT preferred stocks, which add a couple of percent of yield to the portfolio. So right now, RIET is yielding around 12 % on an annualized basis that we pay monthly dividends on.

Rena Sherbill: What else do you feel is pertinent for investors to keep in mind these days when it comes to REITs?

David Auerbach: I think it's important to look through the headlines. You know, good example, Office REITs, because you asked me about Office REITs, Office REITs only represent about 4% or so of a typical index these days, but are garnering 90% of the media headlines and coverage that's out there. I think it's very important to read through the headlines.

I used the analogy before and I will say it again. It is so important to peel back the layers of the onion, get to the core fundamental level of these companies and understand what's going on. One of the things to love about REITs as an investor is what I call the transparency story. Very simple. These companies are very transparent and open vest of

Here's the story, here's our earnings, here's our debt maturity schedule, here's the good, here's the bad, here's our tenant watch list, here's everything you could ever want and then some.

And by the way, if that's not enough, here's our phone number and call us and let's talk to you about what's going on. And at a smaller cap REIT that's run by a handful of guys, the average investor can call investor relations or the company phone number and more likely than not get one of the senior executives on the line.

Because their job, if again, if you understand what I'm talking about here and you're interested, imagine when you talk to a REIT CEO who owns millions of dollars of stock that's entrenched in the front line as a shareholder like you are, that they want to see these good things happen. So headline, there's this commercial real estate wall of death that's coming due. This industry is going underwater.

Realty, go to the company's supplement, go to the debt maturity schedule, look and see, holy cow, okay, there is no debt coming due until 2027. That's still a year, two years down the road. They've got time to work this out versus, oh my gosh, they've got $500 million coming due at the end of December of 25. That's a wall of debt that's coming due that we better address that now. What am I supposed to do?

So I feel like REITs are a perfect example of a sector where there's so many misconceptions and you really have to understand the reality of what's actually going on.

Rena Sherbill: For more reality, check out Hoya Capital on Seeking Alpha and where else can investors get in touch with you, David?

David Auerbach: We're everywhere. We're on LinkedIn, on Twitter. I just launched reittv.com. We're trying to get all the great REIT videos that are out there from knowledge sources such as NAREIT or Cohen and Steers or any REIT interviews on CNBC. But we're very easy to find. We're very easy to get in touch with. And we're always happy to answer any questions that anybody has.

Rena Sherbill: What would you say as we finish this conversation, what would you say is the most misunderstood thing about REITs right now?

David Auerbach: I wasn't expecting that. I got to think about that for a second, you kind of caught me off guard there. Okay, so REITS are impacted by tariffs - I disagree.

I think that as the landlord your job is to rent the space. It's your tenant that might be exposed to tariffs So I think that's one big misunderstanding REITs are interest rate sensitive. I think we went through that.

At the end of the day, REITs are boring and boring is good because REITs are the safety net of your portfolio. They allow you to go out and play these very risky asset classes like bitcoin or some of these prediction markets that are unfolding with REITs. The equity REITs not the mortgage REITs. The equity REITs are bricks and mortars

You can go and observe these properties in real time. How do you observe Nvidia in real time? You can't. You have to wait for days like today when they report their earnings to understand how they're doing. But in a re-owned property, I can drive down the street to the Brixmor (BRX) Shopping Center, look at the parking lot, look at the quality of the product. Is the sign missing a letter? Is there a blinking light on the sign?

Are there 10 going out of business signs on the windows? You can observe a lot with your eyes. And I think that's the cool thing about this industry is that it doesn't matter if you're in Spokane, Washington or South Beach, Miami, coast to coast. You can look at one of tens of thousands of read-owned properties every single day and get a good overall estimate of where things are when it comes to that company.

What I mean is, I mentioned that Brixmor shopping center. That's just one of how many shopping centers does Brixmor own? Well, they also own this, this and this in your backyard. Do you know those properties? What are those properties doing? My gosh, they're always crowded. That's where the H-E-B is. I can't get in there.

So if that's just one example and one little part of your market of something that they own, imagine what they're doing in Lexington, Kentucky, or in Provo, Utah, or wherever it is that that company has properties. A lot of these REITs, like Realty Income or Agree, have properties in every single state across the country. And I think, again, the fact that these REIT management teams, especially their investor relations departments, are very approachable. And you have questions and you want to learn more, pick up the phone, ask the questions. That's what we're here to do.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: S&P 500: 2 Reasons To Buy Call Options
data.description:
VIX index now hovers around average

The thesis of this article is quite straight forward. I will explain why the current market conditions underlying all major equity indices (such as the DJI, SP500, NASDAQ, and/or RTY) offers an attractive setup for investors to buy call options. More specifically, I will explain why this set up can provide an asymmetric risk-reward profile due to the combination of average implied volatility (IV) and the high P/E ratios of these indices. Conventional wisdom suggests high valuations warrant caution or even bearish positioning. However, the market’s implied volatility, as measured by the VIX, is significantly underestimating the potential of an upside surprise of the market in my view. As such, I believe the use of call options can serve as a viable strategy to capture short-term gains with limited capital exposure.

Let me start with the volatility index for the SP500 index. As shown in the next chart, after a few days elevated VIX, the current reading has eased to 19.68, only slightly above the 52-week average of 18.85. The VIX index has spiked above 25 a few times in the past year (or about 1/3 above the average value). The recent spike was largely caused by the market’s anxiety about Nvidia’s Q3 earnings report (ER). As to be detailed in a later section, the volatility of other market indices has also shown similar degrees of easing after NVDA delivered a robust ER. Such a mild volatility helps to decrease the premium for options, making them cost effective strategy, especially when considering the expensive valuation of the market and also the undergoing macroeconomic uncertainties.

Equity valuation is expensive

The current valuation of all market indices is unusually expensive by historical standards in my view. And I believe my assessment applies to all market segments including both large-cap (as represented by the SP500, Dow Jones, or NASDAQ index) and also small caps (e.g., as approximated by the RTY index).

As the first example, the chart below shows S&P 500’s Shiller CAPE Ratio (the Shiller Cyclically Adjusted Price-to-Earnings Ratio). As you can see, the current CAPE ratio is about 40x. This is considerably above long-term average of 17.67x (by a factor or more than 2x). It is also among the most expensive CAPE multiples in the index’s entire history. It is quite close to – by a gap of only ~10% - the highest recorded reading of 44.2x observed at the peak of the dot-com bubble.

The valuation for the NASDAQ 100 index is also very expensive – if not more so than the SP500 as you can see from the next chart below. Note that the CAPE ratio data is taken Siblis Research and the most recent compilation if for the June quarter of 2025. The CAPE ratio of NASDAQ 100 was 52.56x at that time, not only far above the SP500’s reading but also represents a rapid expansion of 53.5% compared to its reading of “only” 34.2x about ~3 years ago. Since the June quarter, the NASDAQ 100 index has further advanced to 24,640 as of this writing, or about 8.6% above the June level. Of course, cooperation earnings have reported growth since the June quarter, but not sufficient to offset the price advancements. As a result, the index CAPE ratio has further expanded since then (to about 55x by my estimate).

From past experiences, the combination of high valuation ratios and average VIX signal investors’ complacency. I believe this is the case here too, where investors are misjudging the true market uncertainties, making the use of option a cost-effective strategy, as detailed next.

Current volatility could understate true uncertainties

In a foregoing section, we examined the VIX index, the IV underlying the SP500. The picture is quite similar for other indies as shown the various panel of the chart below. Here, I am using the corresponding ETFs (SPY, QQQ, and DIA) as proxies to the corresponding indices. As shown in the top panel, SPY’s 30-day rolling volatility is 14.89%, below its 52-week average of 16.53%. Similarly, the volatility for the QQQ ETF currently hovers around 20.62%, also slightly below its historical average of 20.75%. Finally, the blue-chip DIA ETF shows a volatility of 13.11%, noticeably below its historical average of 15.12%.

I believe such muted volatilities represent an underestimate of the true macroeconomic uncertainties in the near term (and I will elaborate more in the final section). Such an underestimation in turn makes the use of call options a cost-effective way to hedge against or even capitalize on a potential surprise with limited capital exposure, and I will examine a few specific ideas immediately below.

Specific option ideas

The next table shows the call option chain for the SPY ETF. These options have an expiry date of Dec 31, 2025. This expiration date is picked to accommodate the next rate decision from the Federal Reserve and also the holiday shopping season, two of the key factors that could make the market fluctuate more than the current IV suggests in my mind.

Now back to the SPY calls. Near-the-money call options are all quite attractively priced in my view thanks to the muted IV. Take a specific example of a call option with a $663 strike price. The option last traded at $17.15. This premium translates into only about 2.5% of the current SPY share price, thus providing investors with exposure with a fraction of the capital layout compared to direct share ownership. These options also provide good liquidity and tradability thanks to the relatively large number of open interest and also trading volume (both in the hundreds).

The next chart shows a very similar idea for QQQ calls. Again, near-the-money call options for QQQ are all quite attractive in my view. Let’s look at the call with $600 strike price in particular. This call last traded at $20.62. This premium translates into about 3.4% of the current QQQ share price, higher than the 2.5% just mentioned above for SPY as QQQ’s IV is higher than SPY. However, when compared to its own historical norm and/or the capital committed required for direct share ownership, the premium is still very cost-effective in my model. And again, these QQQ calls also feature relatively large number of open interest (more than 5,400 for the $600 call) and also trading volume (233 for the $600 call).

Other risks and final thoughts

As aforementioned, I believe the current IV underestimates the true market uncertainties for a variety of reasons. Two of the top factors on my list are consumer behavior in this holiday season and also the Federal Reserve’s rate policy expected in December. The approaching of the holiday season introduces significant retail uncertainty. Consumer spending could lead to substantial revenue surprises, especially among consumer discretionary companies, thereby initiating the exact powerful upward move these call options are structured to capitalize on.

In the meantime, the interest rate outlook has become highly uncertain following the Fed’s latest decision to cut the federal funds rate to 3.75%-4.00%. With the new data (or the lack of) released since that decisions, the outlook for future monetary policy has become highly ambiguous. To wit, the screenshot below is taken from the FedWatch Tool. As seen, the Fed is expected to announce its next rate decision on Dec 10, 2025. The market’s expectation is now split between a “no-cut” scenario (with about 60% odds) and a “25 basis cut” scenario (with about 40% odds). In contrast, the market felt almost certain about a 25-basis cut (with almost 99% certainty) about 1 month ago.

Finally, for investors new to the use of options (either call or put), a word of caution is in order. Buying call options is often considered a leveraged strategy. As such, the positive – as pointed out earlier – is that buyers of call option could gain exposure with relatively small capital commitments in absolute dollar amount. But in terms of percentages, the loss could be – and often are - 100% when/if the call options expire worthlessly.

In conclusion, the goal of this article is to explain why the current equity landscape offers a viable set up for a call option play. The attractiveness of this play is due to two factors: the combination of high valuation ratios and mild volatility. I believe the current volatility is an understate of the fundamental risks brewing beneath the market. As such, I consider call options as an asymmetric bet that favors the buyer, offering exposure with limited downside risk (which is the premium paid) as a few critical macroeconomic forces unfold.

data.title: AAII Sentiment Survey: Pessimism Draws Back
data.description:
Pessimism among individual investors about the short-term outlook for stocks decreased in the latest AAII Sentiment Survey. Meanwhile, optimism and neutral sentiment increased.

Bullish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will rise over the next six months, increased 1.0 percentage points to 32.6%. Bullish sentiment is below its historical average of 37.5% for the fourth time in six weeks.

Neutral sentiment, expectations that stock prices will stay essentially unchanged over the next six months, increased 4.6 percentage points to 23.9%. Neutral sentiment is below its historical average of 31.5% for the 70th time in 72 weeks.

Bearish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will fall over the next six months, decreased 5.6 percentage points to 43.6%. Bearish sentiment is unusually high and is above its historical average of 31.0% for the 51st time in 53 weeks.

The bull-bear spread (bullish minus bearish sentiment) increased 6.5 percentage points to –11.0%. The bull-bear spread is below its historical average of 6.5% for the 38th time in 42 weeks.

This week’s special question asked AAII members how they view the current level of market concentration in mega-cap technology stocks.

Here is how they responded:

  • It’s a major risk that concerns me: 37.5%
  • It’s somewhat concerning, but manageable: 33.2%
  • It’s a natural result of strong business fundamentals: 13.6%
  • It creates good opportunities in other sectors: 13.2%
  • Not sure/no opinion: 2.1%

This week’s Sentiment Survey results:

  • Bullish: 32.6%, up 1.0 percentage points
  • Neutral: 23.9%, up 4.6 percentage points
  • Bearish: 43.6%, down 5.6 percentage points

Historical averages:

  • Bullish: 37.5%
  • Neutral: 31.5%
  • Bearish: 31.0%

The AAII Sentiment Survey has been conducted weekly since July 1987. The survey and its results are available online.

data.title: Lumexa Imaging Seeks IPO To Pay Down Debt
data.description:
Lumexa Is Producing Quality Growth

Lumexa Imaging Holdings, Inc. (LMRI) has filed to raise debt repayment capital in an IPO of its common stock, according to SEC S-1 registration information.

LMRI provides diagnostic imaging services to patients in the United States.

While the company's operating results have improved, the IPO’s proceeds will go to pay off debt, typical for a private equity-owned firm at IPO.

Many of these PE-owned firms have performed in a lackluster manner post-IPO, but perhaps Lumexa may break the pattern.

I’ll provide a final opinion when we learn more IPO details from management.

What Does Lumexa Do?

Raleigh, North Carolina-based Lumexa Imaging Holdings, Inc. was founded to develop a network of imaging diagnostic centers covering numerous US state geographies.

The firm was originally created as a joint venture between Charlotte Radiology and private equity firm Welsh, Carson, Anderson and Stowe [WCAS], and was renamed US Radiology Specialists. It was later rebranded as Lumexa Imaging in July 2025.

It has 99 consolidated locations, comprising either VIEs (Variable Interest Entities) or wholly-owned subsidiaries, with 48 and 51 locations, respectively, as of September 30, 2025.

There are also 85 joint venture locations with health systems, which are unconsolidated affiliates accounted for via the equity method.

Management is led by Chief Executive Officer Ms. Caitlin Zulla, who has been with the firm since January 2025 and was previously CEO of SCA Health, an operator of outpatient surgery locations.

The company's primary offerings include:

  • MRI imaging

  • CT imaging

  • Other services

Lumexa generates its revenue from sources according to the pie chart breakdown below:

For the nine months ended Sept. 30, 2025, advanced imaging represented 52% of its consolidated revenue and 63% of system-wide revenue.

A map of the company's footprint and system metrics is shown here:

Lumexa has booked fair market value investment of $1.5 billion as of September 30, 2025 from investors, including private equity firm Welsh, Carson, Anderson & Stowe and Charlotte Radiology Investors, LLC.

General & Administrative expenses as a percentage of total revenue have trended higher as revenues have grown, as the figures below indicate:

What Is Lumexa’s Market?

According to a 2025 market research report by Fortune Business Insights, the US market for diagnostic imaging services was an estimated $140 billion in 2024 and was projected to reach nearly $240 billion by 2032.

This represents a projected CAGR (Compound Annual Growth Rate) of 7.0% from 2025 to 2032, a reasonably robust growth expectation given the already large size of the market.

The primary factors driving this expected growth are an aging population driving growing demand, coupled with improving technological offerings and a widening application space for medical imaging.

Also, the footprint of diagnostic centers is expected to drive convenience and related growth.

Declining reimbursement rates combined with rising equipment prices may place greater financial strains on smaller healthcare providers, potentially creating demand for lower-cost providers such as Lumexa.

Also, the graphic below illustrates the market dynamics for the US diagnostic imaging services market.

Major competitive vendors or industry players include the following firms:

  • RadNet (RDNT)

  • Akumin (OTCPK:AKUMQ)

  • Rayus Radiology

  • MedQuest Associates

  • LucidHealth

  • Radiology Partners

  • Envision Radiology

  • Capitol Imaging Services

The company markets its services via an in-house sales force of more than 120 sales representatives to physician groups and health systems in the US.

Lumexa seeks to position itself to insurance payors as a cost-effective alternative to hospital-based imaging.

Below is a thumbnail SWOT analysis for Lumexa:

Strengths

  • Large multi-state network of imaging centers, integrated technology platform, substantial network of subspecialized radiologists, longstanding joint venture relationships

Weaknesses

  • High fixed cost structure, heavy dependence on contracted radiology groups, meaningful indebtedness

Opportunities

  • Growing population of aged persons in the US, lower cost structure than hospital imaging, more joint ventures with health systems lacking imaging partners

Threats

  • Government or commercial payor reimbursement reduction risks, shortage of qualified radiologists, regulatory changes and high competition from hospitals or physician practices that acquire their own equipment

Lumexa’s Recent Financial Results

The firm’s recent financial performance is summarized as shown below:

  • Moderately growing topline revenue

  • Increasing gross profit and gross margin

  • Growing operating profit

  • A swing to slight cash used in operations

As of September 30, 2025, Lumexa had $191,000 in cash and equivalents and $1.5 billion in total liabilities, of which debt totalled $1.2 billion.

The company has generated free cash flow for the twelve months ended September 30, 2025, of $1.3 million.

The firm’s performance as a percentage of revenue is shown in the chart below:

The chart indicates that gross margin has improved materially in the first nine months of 2025, while sales & marketing expenses as a percentage of revenue have remained flat, indicating increasing operating leverage.

Lumexa’s IPO Plan

LMRI intends to sell shares of common stock in an IPO that may reach $200 million in size, not including the sale of customary underwriter options.

No existing or possibly new shareholders have indicated an interest in acquiring shares of the IPO, at least as disclosed in the initial prospectus.

Company, shareholder and executive lock-ups will be for the normal and customary 180 days after the effective date of the prospectus.

According to Lumexa’s most recent regulatory filing, it plans to use the net proceeds as follows:

We intend to use the net proceeds of this offering to pay down a portion of our outstanding borrowings under the Existing Term Loan and for working capital, capital expenditures and other general corporate purposes. At September 30, 2025, we had approximately $1.2 billion outstanding under the Existing Term Loan (which will mature in December 2027), which bears interest at a rate per annum equal to the secured overnight financing rate (“SOFR”), plus 4.75% (where the applicable SOFR rate has a 0.5% floor). As of September 30, 2025, the interest rate on the Existing Term Loan was 9.05%. The Refinancing Term Loan (as defined below) that we expect to incur under the New Credit Agreement following the completion of this offering is expected to be in the range of $800 million to $825 million.

(Source - SEC)

The firm’s presentation for its roadshow is not yet available.

Pertaining to potential legal exposure, leadership said the company had no legal exposure that would have a material adverse effect on its financial condition or operations.

Listed underwriters of the IPO are Barclays, J.P. Morgan, Jefferies, Deutsche Bank Securities, Wells Fargo Securities, Leerink Partners, William Blair, Capital One Securities, Fifth Third Securities, Raymond James, PNC Capital Markets LLC, Academy Securities, Loop Capital Markets and R. Seelaus & Co., LLC.

Lumexa Is Improving Operating Results

LMRI is seeking US public capital market investment to pay down its debt, likely incurred as a result of its relationship with its private equity firm investor WCAS.

The firm’s financials have produced slowly growing topline revenue, growing gross profit and gross margin, increasing operating profit and a swing to slight cash used in operations.

Free cash flow for the twelve months ended September 30, 2025, was only $1.3 million.

General & Administrative expenses as a percentage of total revenue have moved higher as revenue has risen and its General & Administrative efficiency multiple rose to 1.0x in the most recent reporting period.

The firm currently plans to pay no dividends and will likely retain all earnings and available cash for its growth initiatives and working capital needs.

LMRI’s recent capital spending history shows it has spent heavily on capital expenditures as a percentage of its operating cash flow.

The company did not pay any share-based compensation in recent years.

The firm’s growth strategy includes improving same-center growth, building new centers in new and existing geographies, new joint ventures with health systems, and select acquisitions of existing imaging center companies.

The market opportunity for providing diagnostic imaging services in the US is quite large and expected to grow at a reasonably strong rate of growth through 2032, on aging demographics, improving technologies and broadening applications, so the company has positive industry growth dynamics in its favor.

Risks to the company’s outlook as a public firm include its ‘emerging growth company’ status, which allows leadership to disclose less information to shareholders.

LMRI has revenue concentration risks due to its operations covering only 13 states, and a substantial portion of revenue comes from the US federal government.

LMRI has produced improved operating results, and its revenue growth rate has also increased, but remains in the single digits.

With the IPO’s proceeds going to pay down debt, which is typical of a private equity firm-owned company at IPO, that's a less-than-optimal aspect of the IPO.

Many of these types of companies have performed disappointingly post-IPO, as they tend to be slow-growing, debt-heavy firms with little new resources with which to implement their growth plans.

When we learn management’s pricing and valuation assumptions, I’ll provide a final opinion.

Expected IPO Pricing Date: To be announced
